description: Planning artifacts: create a follow-on ALGA plan for the EE-only migration instead of rewriting the original Teams v1 plan.
Planning artifacts: the migration PRD explicitly references `2026-03-07-microsoft-teams-integration-v1` as historical context and dependency context.
Planning artifacts: the migration scratchpad records the CE code locations that currently own Teams behavior.
Planning artifacts: the migration scratchpad records the Entra EE boundary pattern as the implementation precedent.
Planning artifacts: the migration plan treats Teams as unreleased and allows current dev/test Teams data to be discarded.
Planning artifacts: the migration plan treats shared Microsoft profiles as retained shared infrastructure rather than part of the EE extraction.
Planning artifacts: the migration plan defines `teams-integration-ui` as the sole new rollout flag assumed for this work.
Planning artifacts: the migration plan defines a fresh-install target state for CE and a separate fresh-install target state for EE.
Planning artifacts: the migration plan distinguishes migration work from already-complete shared Microsoft profile work.
Planning artifacts: the migration plan requires feature and test IDs that remain stable as the migration checklist evolves.
Availability model: define a single Teams availability helper that combines enterprise edition checks with the tenant-level `teams-integration-ui` flag.
Availability model: the shared Teams availability helper accepts tenant context for server-side runtime checks.
Availability model: the shared Teams availability helper accepts optional user context for flag evaluations that support user-aware targeting.
Availability model: Teams admin UI visibility is driven by the shared availability helper rather than ad hoc local checks.
Availability model: Teams runtime route access is driven by the shared availability helper rather than ad hoc local checks.
Availability model: Teams server action access is driven by the shared availability helper rather than ad hoc local checks.
Availability model: Teams notification delivery checks the shared availability helper before attempting runtime work.
Availability model: CE always resolves Teams availability to disabled regardless of tenant flag state.
Availability model: EE with the feature flag disabled resolves Teams availability to disabled without exposing active behavior.
Availability model: EE with the feature flag enabled and valid tenant context resolves Teams availability to enabled.
Availability model: unavailable states distinguish `ce_unavailable`, `flag_disabled`, and `tenant_not_configured` rather than collapsing everything into one generic failure.
Availability model: Teams settings wrappers can render a disabled-state shell when EE is present but the tenant flag is off.
Availability model: Teams runtime wrappers can return a stable EE-unavailable response when running in CE.
Availability model: Teams runtime wrappers can return a stable disabled response when running in EE with the tenant flag off.
Availability model: Teams availability helpers are imported from a shared non-`use server` module so they are safe in both action and UI code.
Availability model: the migration introduces no second Teams rollout flag for separate runtime/UI behavior.
Availability model: the migration does not repurpose unrelated existing flags such as `teams-v2` for Teams integration ownership gating.
Availability model: the plan records the canonical unavailable copy for CE and disabled-flag states so wrappers are consistent.
Availability model: flag evaluation defaults to disabled when feature-flag infrastructure is unavailable.
Availability model: all new EE Teams entrypoints are required to call the shared availability helper before touching tenant Teams state.
Settings IA: remove the Teams integration surface from the shared `Providers` category.
Settings IA: keep `MicrosoftIntegrationSettings` in the shared `Providers` category because Microsoft profiles remain shared infrastructure.
Settings IA: add the Teams integration surface to the `Communication` category.
Settings IA: keep existing Inbound Email configuration in `Communication` while adding Teams alongside it.
Settings IA: the shared settings page composes the Teams card through an EE-safe wrapper rather than importing the concrete shared Teams component directly.
Settings IA: the Teams settings wrapper only renders a Teams card when edition and flag checks pass.
Settings IA: the Teams settings wrapper renders nothing in CE so Teams is not discoverable in the settings UI.
Settings IA: the Teams settings wrapper renders nothing in EE when the tenant flag is disabled unless an explicit disabled-state shell is intentionally chosen.
Settings IA: the `Communication` tab description is updated so Teams fits the communication/inbox/collaboration grouping rather than provider credentials.
Settings IA: the `Providers` tab description no longer implies that Teams setup belongs there.
Settings IA: the settings integration index no longer exports a CE-shaped Teams settings component.
Settings IA: Teams settings entry points are renamed or wrapped so their ownership clearly indicates EE-only behavior.
Settings IA: the Teams settings UI continues to reference shared Microsoft profiles instead of duplicating Microsoft credential entry.
Settings IA: Teams profile-selection UX remains tenant-admin-only.
Settings IA: no user-scoped Teams profile selector is introduced during the migration.
Settings IA: the Teams settings entry preserves the existing Teams setup concepts such as profile selection, capability status, and package/install state.
Settings IA: Teams settings visibility does not depend on whether a tenant already has Teams data in the database.
Settings IA: the shared settings page no longer imports `TeamsIntegrationSettings` from a CE package path.
Settings IA: EE settings composition uses a wrapper or entry module consistent with the existing Entra pattern.
Settings IA: any Teams-related settings copy that says “Providers” is updated to say “Communication”.
Settings IA: Teams settings tests assert the new category placement instead of the historical provider placement.
Settings IA: Teams-specific settings help text remains scoped to MSP users and does not broaden product scope during the migration.
Settings IA: the migration does not move Microsoft profile management into EE.
Settings IA: the migration does not add a second Teams settings surface in another settings area.
Route boundary: the Teams personal-tab page is converted from a shared active implementation into an EE-owned implementation or EE delegator.
Route boundary: the Teams tab page exports only wrapper code in shared CE paths when a public route boundary must remain present.
Route boundary: the Teams tab page returns an EE-unavailable result or `notFound` style response in CE.
Route boundary: the Teams tab page returns a disabled-state response when running in EE with the tenant flag off.
Route boundary: the Teams tab page delegates into EE code when running in EE with the tenant flag enabled.
Route boundary: Teams bot message routes are converted from shared active implementations into EE-owned implementations or EE delegators.
Route boundary: Teams bot routes return an EE-unavailable response in CE.
Route boundary: Teams bot routes return a disabled response in EE when the tenant flag is off.
Route boundary: Teams bot routes delegate into EE implementations when Teams is enabled.
Route boundary: Teams message-extension query routes are converted from shared active implementations into EE-owned implementations or EE delegators.
Route boundary: Teams message-extension action routes are converted from shared active implementations into EE-owned implementations or EE delegators.
Route boundary: Teams message-extension routes return CE-unavailable responses when invoked from CE.
Route boundary: Teams message-extension routes return disabled responses when invoked from EE with the tenant flag off.
Route boundary: Teams message-extension routes delegate into EE implementations when Teams is enabled.
Route boundary: Teams auth callback routes for tab, bot, and message extension are converted from shared active implementations into EE-owned implementations or EE delegators.
Route boundary: Teams auth callback routes return EE-unavailable responses in CE.
Route boundary: Teams auth callback routes return disabled responses in EE with the tenant flag off.
Route boundary: Teams auth callback routes delegate into EE implementations when Teams is enabled.
Route boundary: Teams package generation and install-status routes are converted from shared active implementations into EE-owned implementations or EE delegators.
Route boundary: Teams package/install routes return EE-unavailable responses in CE.
Route boundary: Teams package/install routes return disabled responses in EE with the tenant flag off.
Route boundary: Teams package/install routes delegate into EE implementations when Teams is enabled.
Route boundary: OPTIONS handling for any CE stubbed Teams API routes remains valid even when the EE implementation is unavailable.
Route boundary: shared route wrappers cache EE dynamic imports safely so repeated requests do not reload modules unnecessarily.
Route boundary: shared route wrappers log EE import failures in a bounded way without exposing stack traces to users.
Route boundary: shared route wrappers do not import EE modules eagerly in CE builds.
Route boundary: the migration documents which Teams routes remain addressable as CE stubs and which become hard 404s.
Route boundary: no remaining shared route directly executes Teams business logic after the migration.
EE ownership: concrete Teams tab rendering logic lives under an EE-owned path after the migration.
EE ownership: concrete Teams bot command handling lives under an EE-owned path after the migration.
EE ownership: concrete Teams message-extension search handling lives under an EE-owned path after the migration.
EE ownership: concrete Teams message-extension action handling lives under an EE-owned path after the migration.
EE ownership: concrete Teams adaptive-card and dialog submit handling lives under an EE-owned path after the migration.
EE ownership: concrete Teams package manifest generation lives under an EE-owned path after the migration.
EE ownership: concrete Teams install-status and package metadata logic lives under an EE-owned path after the migration.
EE ownership: concrete Teams quick-action registry logic lives under an EE-owned path after the migration.
EE ownership: concrete Teams notification delivery logic lives under an EE-owned path after the migration.
EE ownership: concrete Teams deep-link composition used only by Teams runtime surfaces lives under an EE-owned path after the migration.
EE ownership: concrete Teams tenant-context resolution used only by Teams runtime surfaces lives under an EE-owned path after the migration.
EE ownership: concrete Teams auth callback helpers used only by Teams runtime surfaces live under EE-owned paths after the migration.
EE ownership: concrete Teams settings UI lives under an EE-owned path after the migration.
EE ownership: concrete Teams setup state persistence helpers live under an EE-owned path after the migration.
EE ownership: EE Teams components continue to consume shared Microsoft profile services through stable shared interfaces.
EE ownership: EE Teams components continue to consume shared PSA notification/link infrastructure through stable shared interfaces.
EE ownership: EE Teams components continue to consume shared auth/session primitives through stable shared interfaces.
EE ownership: EE Teams modules are named and placed so future engineers can identify them as enterprise-only without inspecting internals.
EE ownership: enterprise-only import aliases or package entrypoints are used consistently for Teams runtime modules.
EE ownership: the migration does not create duplicate EE implementations for Microsoft profile management that already lives in shared code.
EE ownership: the migration does not create a second Teams integration model separate from the existing Teams tenant integration concept.
EE ownership: the migration keeps Teams as one EE integration spanning tab, bot, message extension, and notifications rather than fragmenting it into separate EE subsystems.
EE ownership: the migration keeps shared Microsoft profiles as the only supported credential source for Teams.
EE ownership: the migration does not add channel/chat notification routing or other new product scope while moving code to EE.
Server actions: shared Teams server-action modules stop exporting concrete Teams behavior directly from CE-owned paths.
Server actions: shared Teams server-action entrypoints become EE-unavailable stubs or EE delegators where external imports require a stable path.
Server actions: Teams package actions return EE-unavailable results in CE.
Server actions: Teams package actions return disabled results in EE when the tenant flag is off.
Server actions: Teams package actions delegate into EE implementations when Teams is enabled.
Server actions: Teams settings actions return EE-unavailable results in CE.
Server actions: Teams settings actions return disabled results in EE when the tenant flag is off.
Server actions: Teams settings actions delegate into EE implementations when Teams is enabled.
Server actions: Teams quick-action submit actions return EE-unavailable results in CE.
Server actions: Teams quick-action submit actions return disabled results in EE when the tenant flag is off.
Server actions: Teams quick-action submit actions delegate into EE implementations when Teams is enabled.
Server actions: Teams action modules call the shared Teams availability helper before reading Teams tenant state.
Server actions: Teams action result types remain stable across CE stubs and EE implementations so UI callers do not need edition-specific type handling.
Server actions: runtime constants and helpers needed by both UI and action code live outside `use server` files.
Server actions: CE action wrappers do not pull enterprise-only modules into client bundles.
Server actions: shared action indexes no longer make Teams look like a CE-managed provider integration.
Server actions: any shared consumer-binding helpers remain shared and are not moved into EE just because Teams uses them.
Server actions: Teams-specific provider-resolution helpers used only for Teams runtime behavior are moved out of shared auth action surfaces.
Server actions: Teams action wrappers preserve existing result-shape semantics for success, failure, unavailable, and disabled cases.
Server actions: teams package generation still depends on shared Microsoft profile selection state rather than duplicating profile data.
Server actions: teams settings persistence still enforces tenant-admin permissions after moving to EE.
Server actions: teams action wrappers remain safe to import from settings pages, notification code, and server components without edition leakage.
Server actions: shared CE code no longer uses Teams action modules as a source of reusable runtime constants.
Server actions: the migration explicitly tests the `use server` export shape of migrated Teams action modules.
Server actions: no remaining shared server-action module owns Teams business logic after the migration.
Auth boundary: Teams-specific Microsoft provider resolution used by Teams runtime entrypoints is moved behind EE ownership or EE-safe wrappers.
Auth boundary: shared MSP SSO and non-Teams Microsoft auth paths continue to function without depending on EE Teams modules.
Auth boundary: Teams runtime auth checks still enforce MSP-user-only semantics after moving to EE.
Auth boundary: Teams runtime auth checks still enforce tenant matching after moving to EE.
Auth boundary: Teams runtime auth checks still enforce selected Teams Microsoft profile semantics after moving to EE.
Auth boundary: shared auth indexes no longer expose Teams-specific helpers as if they are CE-owned runtime features.
Auth boundary: Teams reauth URL helpers used only for Teams surfaces are moved behind EE ownership or EE-safe wrappers.
Auth boundary: CE does not attempt Teams auth callback flows after the migration.
Auth boundary: EE flag-off state does not attempt Teams auth callback flows after the migration.
Notification boundary: shared internal notification generation remains the source of truth for payloads and record links.
Notification boundary: Teams notification delivery is moved behind EE ownership or EE-safe wrappers.
Notification boundary: CE notification broadcasting never attempts Teams delivery after the migration.
Notification boundary: EE flag-off notification broadcasting never attempts Teams delivery after the migration.
Notification boundary: EE flag-on notification broadcasting can reach Teams delivery through EE-owned code.
Notification boundary: shared notification broadening does not import concrete Teams delivery modules directly.
Notification boundary: Teams-specific notification templates or feed payload helpers used only for Teams delivery are EE-owned.
Notification boundary: Teams deep-link builders used only for Teams feed delivery are EE-owned or wrapped safely.
Notification boundary: non-Teams notification channels remain unaffected by the Teams EE extraction.
Notification boundary: the migration preserves the simplification cascade that Teams notifications are just another delivery channel for existing notification payloads.
Notification boundary: the migration does not add channel-targeted notification routing while moving delivery to EE.
Notification boundary: failures to load EE Teams notification delivery do not break the broader notification broadcast path.
Notification boundary: CE builds do not include Teams notification runtime by accident through shared notification imports.
Schema ownership: shared migration history no longer contains active ownership of Teams integration tables.
Schema ownership: shared migration history no longer contains active ownership of Teams package metadata columns or tables.
Schema ownership: EE migration history creates the Teams integration tables required for Teams tenant setup.
Schema ownership: EE migration history creates the Teams package metadata storage required for Teams manifest/install status.
Schema ownership: shared migration history continues to create Microsoft profile tables.
Schema ownership: shared migration history continues to create Microsoft profile consumer-binding tables.
Schema ownership: fresh CE installs do not create Teams-specific schema.
Schema ownership: fresh EE installs do create Teams-specific schema.
Schema ownership: CE migration validation asserts the absence of Teams tables.
Schema ownership: EE migration validation asserts the presence of Teams tables.
Schema ownership: Teams table creation remains tenant-scoped and aligned with the existing Teams integration data model after moving to EE.
Schema ownership: Teams package metadata persistence remains tenant-scoped after moving to EE.
Schema ownership: the migration plan does not include a production backfill for current dev/test Teams rows.
Schema ownership: any current shared Teams tables can be dropped or ignored in local/dev reset flows without preservation guarantees.
Schema ownership: the migration notes expected handling for developers whose local databases still have pre-migration shared Teams tables.
Schema ownership: the migration does not move shared Microsoft profile secrets or bindings into EE.
Schema ownership: the migration does not create duplicate copies of Microsoft profile tables inside EE.
Schema ownership: Teams schema tests run against migrated databases rather than source-string-only assertions.
Schema ownership: the plan records whether shared migration files are deleted, tombstoned, or replaced with no-op compatibility markers.
Schema ownership: the final schema boundary leaves no ambiguity about whether Teams is CE or EE at the database layer.
Cleanup: the shared integrations component index no longer exports an active Teams settings component.
Cleanup: the shared integrations settings page no longer imports Teams from a CE-owned component path.
Cleanup: shared route trees no longer contain active Teams business logic in page or API handlers.
Cleanup: shared notifications runtime no longer imports concrete Teams delivery code.
Cleanup: shared auth indexes no longer expose concrete Teams-only runtime modules when a wrapper would suffice.
Cleanup: Teams-specific helpers that are only meaningful for Teams runtime are moved out of generic shared utility files where appropriate.
Cleanup: comments and file names are updated so Teams enterprise ownership is obvious to future maintainers.
Cleanup: no settings copy still tells admins to configure Teams from the Providers tab.
Cleanup: no test names still describe Teams as a Providers-category integration unless they are historical references.
Cleanup: no docs introduced by this migration claim that Teams is available in CE.
Cleanup: no shared package barrel accidentally re-exports enterprise Teams code.
Cleanup: no EE module imports shared Teams code that is itself trying to import back into EE recursively.
Cleanup: stale temporary wrappers created during migration are removed before the work is considered complete.
Compatibility: shared Microsoft profile CRUD still works in CE after the Teams extraction.
Compatibility: shared Microsoft profile CRUD still works in EE after the Teams extraction.
Compatibility: shared default-profile compatibility behavior for email/calendar/MSP SSO remains intact after the Teams extraction.
Compatibility: Teams remains able to bind to a selected Microsoft profile through the shared consumer-binding model after the extraction.
Compatibility: the migration does not change tenant-admin permission requirements for managing Microsoft profiles.
Compatibility: the migration does not change tenant-admin permission requirements for managing Teams setup; it only changes ownership and placement.
Compatibility: CE tenants without Teams remain unaffected in behavior and schema after the extraction.
Compatibility: EE tenants with the feature flag off remain unaffected in behavior while Teams code remains unavailable.
Compatibility: EE tenants with the feature flag on can still use the same Teams v1 surface set after the extraction.
Compatibility: the migration preserves the single-integration mental model for Teams across tab, bot, message extension, and notifications.
Compatibility: the migration does not broaden Teams support to client users, channel-first workflows, or channel-targeted notifications.
Test infrastructure: add or update fixture helpers that can simulate CE, EE flag-off, and EE flag-on states for Teams settings and routes.
Test infrastructure: add or update fixture helpers that can simulate Teams availability checks without requiring live PostHog access.
Test infrastructure: add or update fixture helpers that can simulate fresh CE versus fresh EE migration states.
Test infrastructure: add or update fixture helpers that can simulate Teams-disabled wrapper results consistently across settings, actions, and routes.
Test infrastructure: contract tests are added or updated for settings placement in Communication vs Providers.
Test infrastructure: contract tests are added or updated for route delegation into EE implementations.
Test infrastructure: contract tests are added or updated for CE unavailable responses.
Test infrastructure: contract tests are added or updated for flag-disabled responses.
Test infrastructure: contract tests are added or updated for shared Microsoft profile compatibility after extraction.
Test infrastructure: contract tests are added or updated for schema ownership boundaries between shared and EE migrations.
Test infrastructure: scratchpad runbooks list the focused commands needed to validate settings, routes, actions, and migrations for this extraction.
Acceptance: the migration is not complete until Teams is invisible in CE, visible only in EE Communication when flagged on, and database ownership clearly matches that split.
